---
title: "R Notebook"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
  html_notebook: default
  pdf_document: default
---

```{r packages}
#install.packages("dplyr")
library(dplyr)
#install.packages("ggplot2")
library(ggplot2)
#install.packages("agridat")
library(agridat)
#install.packages("sass")
library(sass)
#install.packages("randomForest")
library(randomForest)
#install.packages("ranger")
library(ranger)
#install.packages("magrittr")
library(magrittr)

```


This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Cmd+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r load the data}
rt_raw = read.csv('data/rt_tidy.csv')
summary(rt_raw)
#unique(rt_raw$ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ)
```

```{r extract data}
#select the most typical ethnicities: white, asian, black
races = filter(rt_raw, ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ %in% c ("white", "Black",  "Hispanic/Latina",  "Hispanic/Latino", "Asian"))
white = filter(races, ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ == "white")
asian = filter(races, ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ == "Asian")
black = filter(races, ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ == "Black")
latino = filter(races, ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ %in% c ("Hispanic/Latina",  "Hispanic/Latino"))

```


```{r test anova}
#test whether white people are quicker in judging white faces


praces = ggplot(data = races, ) + 
  geom_boxplot(mapping = aes(x = ethnicity.y, y = rt))
praces

praces = ggplot(races, aes(x = rt , y = ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΗ, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
praces

#anova
mwhite = aov(rt ~ ethnicity.y, data=white)
summary(mwhite)

#conclusion: generally speaking, there's no self-face advantage, quite the opposite, there seems to be a disadvantage (people are not slower in judging their own faces)


```
#Question:  why is it the case?
Hypothesis1: we have too many irrelevant variables at work here: like face, trait
Hypothesis2: face traits interact with races, leading to a lag in judgment 
Hypothesis3: the white people are not necessarily "white" they may come from other races


#test hypothesis 1
a "zoom in"/vertical approach, narrow the scope of variables taken into consideration

```{r in single trait}

# test hypohtesis 1.1 narrow the focus to one single trait
#------------sociable
#across all groups white people's reaction in general
sociable = filter (races, trait == "sociable")
psociable = ggplot(data = sociable) + 
  geom_boxplot(mapping = aes(x = ethnicity.y, y = rt))
psociable

#white people generally judged slower?
#narrow the scope to one trait, one race

white_sociable = filter(white, trait =="caring")
pwhite_sociable = ggplot(data = white_sociable) + 
  geom_boxplot(mapping = aes(x = ethnicity.y, y = rt))
mwhite_sociable= aov(rt ~ ethnicity.y, data=white_sociable)
mwhite_sociable

#---------caring

#across all groups white people's reaction in general
caring = filter (races, trait == "caring")
pcaring = ggplot(data = caring) + 
  geom_boxplot(mapping = aes(x = ethnicity.y, y = rt))
pcaring

#white people generally judged slower?
#narrow the scope to one trait, one race
white_caring = filter(white, trait =="caring")
pwhite_caring = ggplot(data = white_caring) + 
  geom_boxplot(mapping = aes(x = ethnicity.y, y = rt))
pwhite_caring

#------------traits comparison in white only
ptraitsall = ggplot(races, aes(x = rt , y = trait, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
ptraitsall

ptraitswhite = ggplot(white, aes(x = rt , y = trait, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
ptraitswhite

ptraitsblack = ggplot(black, aes(x = rt , y = trait, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
ptraitsblack

ptraitsasian = ggplot(asian, aes(x = rt , y = trait, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
ptraitsasian


```
discovery: by comparing traits in different races, we can find 
           1) white people are quicker in judging social
           2) black people are quicker in judging trustworthy and aggressive 
           3) asian people are quicker in judging attractiveness 
           that is to say 
tip: to find out what's specific about one race, we need to compare it with other races

```{r single trait and single face}
#test1.2 narrow the scope to one face
#rq: whether asians are quicker in judging one certain face among all asian faces

asianfaces = filter (asian, Race == "A")

pasianfaces= ggplot(asianfaces, aes(x = rt , y = stim_id)) + 
     geom_boxplot()
pasianfaces
#pasianone= ggplot(asianfaces, aes(x = rt , y = trait, fill = ethnicity.y)) + 
#geom_boxplot()


#test1.3 narrow the scope to one face, one trait
asianfacesattractive = filter (asianfaces, trait == "attractive")
pasianfacesattractive= ggplot(asianfacesattractive, aes(x = rt , y = stim_id)) + 
     geom_boxplot()
pasianfacesattractive
#conclusion: not applicable, because faces have different features, so it can't be narrowed to a single trait

#test1.4, a follow-up of 1.2, what distinguishes AM225 from the others, are they are Asian than the rest and 
#        how to set the criteria of "asian-typical"

#????????????????????

#discovery: when the scope is smaller, the result is more varied, why

```
#test hypothesis 2
question: how to dissociate the features of stimulus (gender, ra)
a parallel appraoch, taking different variables into consideration at the same time

```{r anova}


#test 2.1 discreet - continuous?
#     2.1.1 random forest
mforest <- randomForest(rt ~ Race + Gender + Age + trait, data = asian, ntree = 500, mtry = 2, importance = TRUE)
mforest

mranger <- ranger(rt~Race + Gender + Age + trait,num.trees = 500,data = asian, mtry = 2,importance = "impurity")
mranger

temp = select(asian, rt, trait, Race, Gender, Age)
mranger_imp <- data.frame(var=names(temp)[-1],imps = mranger$variable.importance/max(mranger$variable.importance))
mranger_imp %>% 
  ggplot(aes(imps,x=reorder(var,imps)))+
  geom_point(size=3,colour="black")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(x="predictor",y="importance")+
  theme_bw(18)
 
# it turned out that race is the least important variable??????
# possibly because they are not the same types of variable (numeric, categorical), therefore the method is problematic 
# the biggest question is: how to dissociate the factor of race from the rest

#     2.1.2 other statistical methods, anova


#test 2.2 convert discreet into continuous variables 

# test.2.2.1 convert race 
nraces = races
nraces$ethnicity.y[which(nraces$ethnicity.y =='white')] <- '1'
nraces$ethnicity.y[which(nraces$ethnicity.y =='latinx')] <- '2'
nraces$ethnicity.y[which(nraces$ethnicity.y =='asian')] <- '3'
nraces$ethnicity.y[which(nraces$ethnicity.y =='black')] <- '4'
as.numeric(nraces$ethnicity.y)

nraces$trait 
#meaning of numbers: the smaller the number is, the lighter the skin color is

nraces$smallrt = nraces$rt/1000

cor(nraces$ethnicity.y,nraces$smallrt,method="pearson")
ggplot(nraces,aes(x=ethnicity.y,y=smallrt))+ geom_point(size=1,shape=15)+geom_smooth(method=lm)

#even if it is converted into numeric variables, it's still difficult to pinpoint the correlation between the two
# is it necessary to include rating as well

#test 2.2.2 convert traits???



```
